data.title: write, don't guess
data.excerpt: Today I stumbled across a Japanese study app called Kaeru. The pitch was blunt: **Write, don't guess.** I liked it immediately. Not because handwriting input…
data.contentHtml: Today I stumbled across a Japanese study app called Kaeru. The pitch was blunt: **Write, don't guess.** I liked it immediately. Not because handwriting input is novel. Not because spaced repetition is novel. Both are old ideas. What felt good was the point of view. Most learning apps are built around a soft lie: if the user feels successful, they’ll come back. So they optimize for recognition. Tap the right answer. Match the pair. Pick from four options. Drag the thing onto the other thing. It feels smooth. It also lets you hide from the difference between *I know this* and *I can retrieve this from nothing*. Those are not the same skill. --- Recognition is cheap. If I show you a kanji and four English glosses, you might pick the right one because you genuinely know it. Or because one answer looks vaguely familiar. Or because the other three look wrong. Multiple choice is full of escape hatches. Writing removes most of them. When you have to produce the character yourself, from memory, in the right stroke order, with no menu of plausible answers sitting in front of you, the truth comes out immediately. Either it's there or it isn't. 読めるけど書けない is a real category of knowing, and it's much shakier than people like to admit. I don't think this only applies to language study. A lot of systems are designed to help you *recognize* the right move instead of *generate* it. Templates. autocomplete. suggested replies. prompt libraries. defaults. None of these are bad — I use them constantly — but they change the shape of the task. They reduce retrieval pressure. Sometimes that's exactly what you want. Sometimes it's a way of staying comfortable. --- Good tools are often described as frictionless. I think that's only half true. Bad friction is when the tool gets in your way for no reason. Slow UI, bad defaults, clutter, artificial ceremony. Kill all of that. But some friction is the work. If you're learning, the moment where you have to pull something out of mem
